title: Tailwind CSS
summary: Tailwind is a utility first CSS framework.
You style by adding small classes right in your HTML.
Each class does one small thing.
It speeds up building once you learn the class names.

sections.text: Most CSS lives in a separate file with named rules. Tailwind takes a different path. You style elements by stacking tiny purpose built classes directly on them. It feels strange at first, then very fast.

sections.title: Utility First
sections.text: Tailwind gives you a huge set of small classes, each doing one job. One class adds padding, another sets a text color, another makes something a flex container. You combine them on an element to build up its look, without writing custom CSS rules.

sections.title: The Trade Off
sections.text: The benefit is speed and consistency. You stop inventing class names and switching between files, and the spacing and colors come from one shared system. The cost is that your HTML gets longer and busier, which takes some getting used to.

media.alt: An element with several small Tailwind classes building up its look

sections.title: Building with Small Bricks
sections.text: Writing custom CSS is like molding a single custom shape for each part. Tailwind is like building with small standard bricks. Each brick is simple and does one thing, but snap enough of them together and you can build almost anything, quickly.

media.alt: Small standard bricks snapping together into a finished shape

sections.title: Styling with Tailwind
items: Pick the element you want to style.
Add small classes for spacing, color, and layout.
Combine as many classes as the look needs.
Reuse the same class names across the whole project.

sections.text: Tailwind scans your files and removes every class you did not use, so the final CSS it ships is usually tiny, even though the full set of classes is enormous.

terms.definition: A small class that does one styling job.
A ready made toolkit you build on top of.
Shared spacing and colors across the project.
Styling mainly by combining small classes.

questions.text: How do you style with Tailwind?
options: By adding small classes in the HTML
By writing one big rule per page
questions.explanation: With Tailwind you style by adding small utility classes directly on the element.

questions.text: What does each Tailwind class do?
questions.explanation: Each Tailwind utility class does one small styling job.

options: The HTML gets longer and busier
questions.explanation: The cost of Tailwind is that your HTML becomes longer and busier with classes.
